---
title: "The Dirac delta function"
---

::: {.hidden}
 \providecommand{\vv}[1]{{\mathbf{#1}}}
 \providecommand{\pd}[2]{{\frac{\partial #1}{\partial #2}}}
:::

In order to take Fourier transforms of periodic functions such as sines and cosines, we need to introduce the *Dirac delta*, denoted $\delta(x)$. This is not a function in the usual sense, but a **distribution**: it is defined by how it behaves inside integrals.

Informally, $\delta(x)$ is concentrated at $x=0$, is zero away from $0$, and has total mass $1$:
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(x)\,dx = 1.
$$
In fact, it integrates to 1 when the interval contains $x=0$, and integrates to zero otherwise. You can think of it as a very high, very narrow spike around $x=0$.

Its most important property is the **sifting property**:
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,\delta(x-a)\,dx = f(a),
$$
for any function $f$ that is continuous at $x=a$. In particular,
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,\delta(x)\,dx = f(0).
$$

We will also need the *Heaviside step function*, denoted $H(x)$, which is defined by
$$
H(x) =
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } x < 0, \\
1 & \text{if } x \geq 0.
\end{cases}
$$

By the definitions above,
$$
\int_{-\infty}^x \delta(x')\,dx' = H(x).
$$
So the delta distribution can be thought of as the derivative of the Heaviside function
$$
\delta(x) = \frac{d}{dx}H(x).
$$

The delta distribution is often used to model point sources or impulses. In Fourier analysis, it is especially useful because it lets us describe the Fourier transforms of periodic functions.

## Fourier transform of sines and cosines

Recall our Fourier transform convention:
$$
\hat{f}(k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)e^{-ikx}\,dx,
$$
with inverse transform
$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{f}(k)e^{ikx}\,dk.
$$

We now find the Fourier transform of $f(x)=\sin(ax)$. Since
$$
\sin(ax) = \frac{1}{2i}e^{iax} - \frac{1}{2i}e^{-iax},
$$
we seek a distribution $\hat{f}(k)$ such that
$$
\frac{1}{2i}e^{iax} - \frac{1}{2i}e^{-iax}
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{f}(k)e^{ikx}\,dk.
$$

Using the sifting property of the delta distribution, we see that this is achieved by
$$
\hat{f}(k) = \frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2i}\delta(k-a) - \frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2i}\delta(k+a).
$$

Indeed,
$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
\left(
\frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2i}\delta(k-a) - \frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2i}\delta(k+a)
\right)e^{ikx}\,dk
=
\frac{1}{2i}e^{iax} - \frac{1}{2i}e^{-iax}
=
\sin(ax).
$$

A similar calculation gives the Fourier transform of $\cos(ax)$:
$$
\hat{f}(k) = \frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2}\delta(k-a) + \frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2}\delta(k+a).
$$

::: {.callout-important}
With this Fourier transform convention,
$$
\widehat{\sin(ax)}(k)
=
\frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2i}\delta(k-a)
-
\frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2i}\delta(k+a),
$$
and
$$
\widehat{\cos(ax)}(k)
=
\frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2}\delta(k-a)
+
\frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2}\delta(k+a).
$$
:::
